category_summary:
The day was dominated by **Google's** [launch of **Gemini 3.1 Pro**](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-3838a20a9f50), announced across multiple executives including **Demis Hassabis**, **Jeff Dean**, and **Noam Shazeer**. The model [scored 77.1% on ARC-AGI-2](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-2a584eed38f4) — more than doubling **Gemini 3 Pro** — with **Perplexity** CEO **Arav Srinivas** [confirming it as second most-picked](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-c757aa8ac7a6) engine behind **Claude 4.5** on their platform.

- **Andrej Karpathy** [laid out a compelling vision](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-7b2a0740f63e) of ephemeral, AI-generated bespoke software replacing traditional app stores, drawing 905K views
- **François Chollet** [offered a conceptual breakthrough](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-d92d26c99920) framing agentic coding as analogous to machine learning — specs as loss functions, agents as optimizers, codebases as opaque models
- **Jeremy Howard** [flagged a serious security vulnerability](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-4f76aaa45842): LLMs calling tools outside their allowed list, affecting all major US providers except **OpenAI**
- **Jerry Liu** [shared **LlamaIndex's** internal memo](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-27fa922d6711) revealing coding agents have reduced implementation costs to near-zero, reshaping engineering team structures
- A biosecurity RCT study [surprised forecasters](/?date=2026-02-20&category=social#item-08cca97f1a69) by showing mid-2025 LLMs provided **no meaningful uplift** for novices attempting wet lab tasks, a significant AI safety finding

Meanwhile, strong negative sentiment swirled around **Anthropic**, with **Scobleizer's** blunt "Anthropic really fumbled" post hitting nearly 1M views — likely tied to **Claude Sonnet 4.6** backlash.

items.title:
Very interested in what the coming era of highly bespoke software might look like.

Example from thi...
items.content:
Very interested in what the coming era of highly bespoke software might look like.

Example from this morning - I've become a bit loosy goosy with my cardio recently so I decided to do a more srs, regimented experiment to try to lower my Resting Heart Rate from 50 -> 45, over experiment duration of 8 weeks. The primary way to do this is to aspire to a certain sum total minute goals in Zone 2 cardio and 1 HIIT/week.

1 hour later I vibe coded this super custom dashboard for this very specific experiment that shows me how I'm tracking. Claude had to reverse engineer the Woodway treadmill cloud API to pull raw data, process, filter, debug it and create a web UI frontend to track the experiment. It wasn't a fully smooth experience and I had to notice and ask to fix bugs e.g. it screwed up metric vs. imperial system units and it screwed up on the calendar matching up days to dates etc.

But I still feel like the overall direction is clear:
1) There will never be (and shouldn't be) a specific app on the app store for this kind of thing. I shouldn't have to look for, download and use some kind of a "Cardio experiment tracker", when this thing is ~300 lines of code that an LLM agent will give you in seconds. The idea of an "app store" of a long tail of discrete set of apps you choose from feels somehow wrong and outdated when LLM agents can improvise the app on the spot and just for you.
2) Second, the industry has to reconfigure into a set of services of sensors and actuators with agent native ergonomics. My Woodway treadmill is a sensor - it turns physical state into digital knowledge. It shouldn't maintain some human-readable frontend and my LLM agent shouldn't have to reverse engineer it, it should be an API/CLI easily usable by my agent. I'm a little bit disappointed (and my timelines are correspondingly slower) with how slowly this progression is happening in the industry overall. 99% of products/services still don't have an AI-native CLI yet. 99% of products/services maintain .html/.css docs like I won't immediately look for how to copy paste the whole thing to my agent to get something done. They give you a list of instructions on a webpage to open this or that url and click here or there to do a thing. In 2026. What am I a computer? You do it. Or have my agent do it.

So anyway today I am impressed that this random thing took 1 hour (it would have been ~10 hours 2 years ago). But what excites me more is thinking through how this really should have been 1 minute tops. What has to be in place so that it would be 1 minute? So that I could simply say "Hi can you help me track my cardio over the next 8 weeks", and after a very brief Q&A the app would be up. The AI would already have a lot personal context, it would gather the extra needed data, it would reference and search related skill libraries, and maintain all my little apps/automations.

TLDR the "app store" of a set of discrete apps that you choose from is an increasingly outdated concept all by itself. The future are services of AI-native sensors & actuators orchestrated via LLM glue into highly custom, ephemeral apps. It's just not here yet.

items.content_html:
Very interested in what the coming era of highly bespoke software might look like.

Example from this morning - I've become a bit loosy goosy with my cardio recently so I decided to do a more srs, regimented experiment to try to lower my Resting Heart Rate from 50 -> 45, over experiment duration of 8 weeks. The primary way to do this is to aspire to a certain sum total minute goals in Zone 2 cardio and 1 HIIT/week.

1 hour later I vibe coded this super custom dashboard for this very specific experiment that shows me how I'm tracking. Claude had to reverse engineer the Woodway treadmill cloud API to pull raw data, process, filter, debug it and create a web UI frontend to track the experiment. It wasn't a fully smooth experience and I had to notice and ask to fix bugs e.g. it screwed up metric vs. imperial system units and it screwed up on the calendar matching up days to dates etc.

But I still feel like the overall direction is clear:

1) There will never be (and shouldn't be) a specific app on the app store for this kind of thing. I shouldn't have to look for, download and use some kind of a "Cardio experiment tracker", when this thing is ~300 lines of code that an LLM agent will give you in seconds. The idea of an "app store" of a long tail of discrete set of apps you choose from feels somehow wrong and outdated when LLM agents can improvise the app on the spot and just for you.

2) Second, the industry has to reconfigure into a set of services of sensors and actuators with agent native ergonomics. My Woodway treadmill is a sensor - it turns physical state into digital knowledge. It shouldn't maintain some human-readable frontend and my LLM agent shouldn't have to reverse engineer it, it should be an API/CLI easily usable by my agent. I'm a little bit disappointed (and my timelines are correspondingly slower) with how slowly this progression is happening in the industry overall. 99% of products/services still don't have an AI-native CLI yet. 99% of products/services maintain .html/.css docs like I won't immediately look for how to copy paste the whole thing to my agent to get something done. They give you a list of instructions on a webpage to open this or that url and click here or there to do a thing. In 2026. What am I a computer? You do it. Or have my agent do it.

So anyway today I am impressed that this random thing took 1 hour (it would have been ~10 hours 2 years ago). But what excites me more is thinking through how this really should have been 1 minute tops. What has to be in place so that it would be 1 minute? So that I could simply say "Hi can you help me track my cardio over the next 8 weeks", and after a very brief Q&A the app would be up. The AI would already have a lot personal context, it would gather the extra needed data, it would reference and search related skill libraries, and maintain all my little apps/automations.

TLDR the "app store" of a set of discrete apps that you choose from is an increasingly outdated concept all by itself. The future are services of AI-native sensors & actuators orchestrated via LLM glue into highly custom, ephemeral apps. It's just not here yet.

items.title: Sufficiently advanced agentic coding is essentially machine learning: the engineer sets up the optim...
items.content:
Sufficiently advanced agentic coding is essentially machine learning: the engineer sets up the optimization goal as well as some constraints on the search space (the spec and its tests), then an optimization process (coding agents) iterates until the goal is reached.

The result is a blackbox model (the generated codebase): an artifact that performs the task, that you deploy without ever inspecting its internal logic, just as we ignore individual weights in a neural network.

This implies that all classic issues encountered in ML will soon become problems for agentic coding: overfitting to the spec, Clever Hans shortcuts that don't generalize outside the tests, data leakage, concept drift, etc.

I would also ask: what will be the Keras of agentic coding? What will be the optimal set of high-level abstractions that allow humans to steer codebase 'training' with minimal cognitive overhead?
items.author: @fchollet

Predicts classic ML problems (overfitting, shortcuts, data leakage) will plague agentic coding. Asks what will be the 'Keras of agentic coding.'
items.reasoning: Brilliant, original conceptual framework from one of the most respected AI researchers. 180K views, 2.4K likes. Connects two major trends (agentic coding and ML) in a novel way with concrete predictions. Will likely be widely cited/discussed.

items.title: Piotr discovered something worrying: if you give an LLM a list of tools it's allowed to call, it mig...
items.content:
Piotr discovered something worrying: if you give an LLM a list of tools it's allowed to call, it might decide to also call a tool you didn't provide!

Impacts all major US providers except @OpenAI.

Be sure to check LLM tool call requests! (Lisette/Claudette check automatically)

items.title: Coding agents are fundamentally changing software engineering in terms of velocity, role, and org st...
items.content:
Coding agents are fundamentally changing software engineering in terms of velocity, role, and org structure.

We published a memo to our internal engineering team detailing our growing expectations in terms of role/scope.

🟠 Before, the tasks of prioritization, engineering planning, and implementation were divided between EMs, PMs, senior ICs, and junior ICs
🟢 Now, ICs are expected to handle *all* of product prioritization, product speccing, and implementation

This is due to a few trends 📈:
- Coding agents have brought implementation costs down to ~0. The role of engineers is writing prompts
- LLMs and sub-agents have reduced the PM work of synthesizing feedback down to ~0 too

The main job of any “engineer” is to be an e2e product owner: being able to translate requirements into specifications, and delegate tasks to various subagents for implementation.

Every engineer is told to offload as much as possible to their favorite tools, whether it’s Claude Code, Cursor, Devin, Codex, regular ChatGPT and more.
We celebrate and share learnings around burning tokens, as long as it helps drive additional productivity!

Encourages burning tokens across Claude Code, Cursor, Devin, Codex, etc.
items.content_html:
Coding agents are fundamentally changing software engineering in terms of velocity, role, and org structure.

We published a memo to our internal engineering team detailing our growing expectations in terms of role/scope.

🟠 Before, the tasks of prioritization, engineering planning, and implementation were divided between EMs, PMs, senior ICs, and junior ICs

🟢 Now, ICs are expected to handle *all* of product prioritization, product speccing, and implementation

This is due to a few trends 📈:

  • Coding agents have brought implementation costs down to ~0. The role of engineers is writing prompts
  • LLMs and sub-agents have reduced the PM work of synthesizing feedback down to ~0 too

The main job of any “engineer” is to be an e2e product owner: being able to translate requirements into specifications, and delegate tasks to various subagents for implementation.

Every engineer is told to offload as much as possible to their favorite tools, whether it’s Claude Code, Cursor, Devin, Codex, regular ChatGPT and more.

We celebrate and share learnings around burning tokens, as long as it helps drive additional productivity!

items.title:
💬 Having each of your sites/apps as a chat is such a logical idea once you do it

It can message you...
items.content:
💬 Having each of your sites/apps as a chat is such a logical idea once you do it

It can message you if there's an error (I had this for years already, every server, PHP or JS error or unexpected problem ends up in the chat so I can fix it fast)

But now being able to message it back and reply to those errors "ok fix it" and it ending up in Claude Code that immediately fixes it is makes it two-way

Or just messaging it to build new features while you're going throughout the day (having coffee, lunch, going to the gym etc)

It means I will finally be able to do my work on my iPhone from anywhere in the world

Which @pmarca predicted 12 years ago would happen

items.title: this might be the single best timed called shot in the history of AIE. I now think about this talk a...
items.content:
this might be the single best timed called shot in the history of AIE. I now think about this talk almost ~daily and have direct line of sight to the nonstop onslaught of new post-IDE form factors for agentic engineering.

@RealGeneKim  and @Steve_Yegge really nailed this one, no notes. and they called out one of the predominant shifts in 2026 coding, in Nov 2025. i'm still shocked how -I- myself have changed opinion this dramatically in the last 3 months, because unlike appearances I'm actually not an early adopter of things personally (although I serve early adopters for a living). So by the time -I, a perennially left-of-mid-bell-curve person- have come around to the idea, then you really, really know its here.

well: it's here. @Wattenberger just showed me what she and the Augment team have been cooking and yeah, this is the "ADE" or whatever three letter acronym you wanna call it. Cursor 2.0 was a toe dip. Claude folded it into their chat app. Codex formalized the Conductor patterns. Amazon Kiro went hard on Spec Driven Dev. but Intent... this feels like every good idea i've heard in code agent management rolled into one app that, very generously, does not lock you into only using Augment's inhouse coding agent.

I'm in awe at all these smart people I get to talk to because yeah the future of how software is made is happening right in front of my eyes and people will ask us what it was like during this golden age for the rest of our lives.

items.title:
📊 The RCT results surprised most forecasters.

Here's how the median forecasts compare to the RCT re...
items.content:
📊 The RCT results surprised most forecasters.

Here's how the median forecasts compare to the RCT results. (Percentages are the share of novices in each arm that completed all three core wet lab tasks).

🌐 Internet access only

Median forecast: 12%
RCT result: 7%

🤖 Internet access + LLMs

Median forecast: 27%
RCT result: 5%

In other words, forecasters predicted significant wet lab skill uplift from mid-2025 LLMs, but the RCT showed no statistically significant effect.

Superforecasters predictions were closest to the true value, followed by biosecurity experts and then virology experts.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: Following yesterday's [Research](/?date=2026-02-19&category=research#item-7a2d9fa43e1f) publication, Key RCT finding: Forecasters predicted 27% of novices with LLM access could complete wet lab tasks vs 12% without, but actual results showed 5% with LLMs vs 7% without - NO significant uplift from mid-2025 LLMs for wet lab skills. Superforecasters were most accurate.
items.reasoning: Highly significant biosecurity finding: mid-2025 LLMs did NOT provide meaningful wet lab skill uplift to novices, contradicting expert predictions. This is important evidence for AI safety/biorisk policy debates. Superforecasters outperformed domain experts.

Superforecasters were most accurate.

items.title: This will be a huge issue. The *benefits* of AI are right now *EXTREMELY* geographically concentrate...
items.content: This will be a huge issue. The *benefits* of AI are right now *EXTREMELY* geographically concentrated in a few IT/knowledge work supercenters. While the infrastructure is VERY widely geographically distributed. I don’t buy into much of the anti-datacenter fearmongering, but the fact stands that whenever you have such highly skewed benefit/impact distribution, you end up - at a minimum - with high potential for social unrest.

3.1 Pro represents a major step forward in core reasoning. It scored 7...
items.content:
Introducing Gemini 3.1 Pro 🚀

3.1 Pro represents a major step forward in core reasoning. It scored 77.1% (more than doubling 3 Pro’s score) on ARC-AGI-2, the benchmark that evaluates a model's ability to solve new logic patterns and work through challenges it hasn’t encountered before.

This demo illustrates how the model can go beyond the prompt. Instead of rendering a video or static graphic, 3.1 Pro codes a full environment, integrating generative audio and providing UI controls.

Like they really wouldn't care about world peace when it comes down to it, only winning?

It's kinda freaky to me, I mean they are winning, but I don't know, you have to remember regardless of whatever level you're playing at (and they're playing at the highest level), entrepreneurship is still just a game? You can't take it so serious that you won't hold hands with your competitor right?

Sundar being nice here only makes me more positive about Google

Each of my sites (almost, I'm still migrating) is now on t...
items.content:
No because I talk directly with my sites

Each of my sites (almost, I'm still migrating) is now on their own Hetzner VPS

Like https://t.co/2R4tRNJF0z for example, on that VPS I have Claude Code and https://t.co/wNWBUhh9QZ

I asked Claude Code to install that as a system daemon (so it starts on boot, and auto restarts if quits)

A message comes in, it opens a Claude Code that resumes the previous session, writes whatever I said on Telegram and once Claude Code responds it sends it back to Telegram

Very simple

items.title:
Increased thinking doesn't correlate with increased document understanding 

Models are getting much...
items.content:
Increased thinking doesn't correlate with increased document understanding 

Models are getting much better at reasoning which translates well for math/coding/general intelligence tasks. We did some experiments with GPT-5.2 on different thinking modes to see if it actually helps improves scores on OmniDocBench.

Result: it doesn't, and in fact the higher the thinking, the more the output structure might deviate from the existing input. 

Shoutout to Boyang on the @llama_index team for this blog. Come check out our post! 

https://t.co/DBJB8fGSBo
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu shares LlamaIndex research finding that increased thinking/reasoning mode in GPT-5.2 does NOT improve document understanding on OmniDocBench - and higher thinking can actually deviate output structure from input.
items.reasoning: Original research finding with practical implications: reasoning improvements don't transfer to document understanding tasks. Challenges assumption that more thinking = universally better. Technical depth from a credible source.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu shares LlamaIndex research finding that increased thinking/reasoning mode in GPT-5.2 does NOT improve document understanding on OmniDocBench - and higher thinking can actually deviate output structure from input.
items.content_html:
Increased thinking doesn't correlate with increased document understanding

Models are getting much better at reasoning which translates well for math/coding/general intelligence tasks. We did some experiments with GPT-5.2 on different thinking modes to see if it actually helps improves scores on OmniDocBench.

Result: it doesn't, and in fact the higher the thinking, the more the output structure might deviate from the existing input.

Shoutout to Boyang on the @llama_index team for this blog. Come check out our post!

https://t.co/DBJB8fGSBo

items.title: This is why these benchmark improvements are all crap. Gemini 3.1 has marginal improvements compared...
items.content:
This is why these benchmark improvements are all crap. Gemini 3.1 has marginal improvements compared to the competition on all benchmarks but one: Useless-Puzzles-2.

This means only one thing: they have finetuned 3.1 Pro specifically to improve on this single benchmark without modifying the underlying architecture.

Tomorrow, someone releases another useless benchmark, and Gemini will suck on it until the benchmark becomes hyped and they add the training data for this benchmark to the finetuning dataset.

So boring.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov aggressively critiques Gemini 3.1 Pro, arguing the ARC-AGI-2 score is the only meaningful improvement and was achieved through benchmark-specific finetuning rather than real architectural advancement.
items.reasoning: Most substantive contrarian analysis of the day's biggest launch. 46K views, 331 likes. Whether correct or not, forces the community to think critically about benchmark-driven claims.
items.summary_html: Burkov aggressively critiques Gemini 3.1 Pro, arguing the ARC-AGI-2 score is the only meaningful improvement and was achieved through benchmark-specific finetuning rather than real architectural advancement.
items.content_html:
This is why these benchmark improvements are all crap. Gemini 3.1 has marginal improvements compared to the competition on all benchmarks but one: Useless-Puzzles-2.

This means only one thing: they have finetuned 3.1 Pro specifically to improve on this single benchmark without modifying the underlying architecture.

Tomorrow, someone releases another useless benchmark, and Gemini will suck on it until the benchmark becomes hyped and they add the training data for this benchmark to the finetuning dataset.

So boring.

items.title: Not that odd if you really understand how *human* intelligence works. We’ve known about the existenc...
items.content: Not that odd if you really understand how *human* intelligence works. We’ve known about the existence of the “g-factor” for about a century now. If *all* of the LLM training data is downstream from human cognitive abilities, then it’s intuitively unsurprising that these abilities would be highly correlated.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz connects LLM capability correlations to the psychological 'g-factor' of human intelligence, arguing it's unsurprising that LLM abilities correlate since all training data derives from human cognition.
items.reasoning: Genuinely insightful observation connecting psychometrics (g-factor) to LLM capabilities. High engagement (70 likes, 9.2K views). Original intellectual framework that adds to understanding of why LLMs show correlated capabilities across domains.
items.summary_html: Tunguz connects LLM capability correlations to the psychological 'g-factor' of human intelligence, arguing it's unsurprising that LLM abilities correlate since all training data derives from human cognition.
items.content_html: Not that odd if you really understand how *human* intelligence works. We’ve known about the existence of the “g-factor” for about a century now. If *all* of the LLM training data is downstream from human cognitive abilities, then it’s intuitively unsurprising that these abilities would be highly correlated.

items.title: In mid-2025, @ActiveSiteBio told us about an RCT it was working on that would test how access to LLM...
items.content:
In mid-2025, @ActiveSiteBio told us about an RCT it was working on that would test how access to LLMs affected novices' ability to do molecular biology work in a wet lab.

We ran a forecasting study alongside this RCT, to find out what forecasters predicted for the RCT outcome and to understand how the study results connected to real world biorisk.

So, how well did superforecasters and experts predict wet lab skill uplift from LLMs? 

🧵 Read on to find out…

items.summary: FRI thread opener: announcing results of a forecasting study run alongside Active Site's RCT testing whether LLMs help novices perform molecular biology work in wet labs

I don’t see why “taste” and direction are uniquely human, like many people say.

If an AI can train on it, it can learn it.

items.title:
My AI read 60,000 posts and analyzed them for discussion of Kling and found:

1. AI video is enterin...
items.content:
My AI read 60,000 posts and analyzed them for discussion of Kling and found:

1. AI video is entering the "cinema" phase — Moving from single clips to multi-shot narratives with audio. Kling 3.0 is the first to demonstrate this at scale.

2. China is competitive in generative video — Kuaishou ($240M ARR), ByteDance (Seedance), Alibaba (Wan 2.6), MiniMax. Chinese models are matching or exceeding US alternatives in video generation.

3. The "AI Director" concept is real — @AIVideoDotCom: "The race to build the AI Director is officially on." Multi-shot + audio + character consistency = automated filmmaking pipeline.

4. Pricing democratizes access — Free tier (66 daily credits) + $6.99 entry means anyone can make AI videos. Compare to traditional video production costs of $1,000-50,000.

5. Platform ecosystem is the moat — Kling launched 3.0 on 8+ platforms simultaneously. Distribution > raw model quality.

6. Creator community drives adoption — Short films going viral (711 RTs, 1.86M views) create a flywheel of awareness → adoption → content → more awareness.

7. Audio-visual generation is the new standard — Silent AI video is already feeling outdated. Every major model will need native audio.

8. Motion Control is underappreciated — Reference video motion transfer is unique to Kling and could become the standard for directing AI video.

So, why haven't you tried making a video in Kling? It's easy, it's fast, it doesn't cost much, and the quality is amazing.

Try it at https://t.co/RGwbqkLd8Z

items.title: 🔮 Although the Active Site RCT did not show uplift in mid-2025, we were interested in how people wou...
items.content:
🔮 Although the Active Site RCT did not show uplift in mid-2025, we were interested in how people would react if future work discovered a larger effect.

We asked forecasters to imagine a repeat of the Active Site RCT in 2026, yielding various hypothetical results

The below chart shows how their outbreak risk estimate changed depending on the hypothetical RCT results.

The resulting “risk curves” sloped upwards for all forecaster groups, indicating that participants perceived the RCT as a relevant signal of increased outbreak risk.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI forecasting study: researchers asked forecasters how hypothetical future RCT results showing LLM wet lab uplift would change their outbreak risk estimates. Risk curves sloped upward for all groups.
items.reasoning: Important biosecurity research connecting LLM capability to outbreak risk perception. Methodologically interesting forecasting approach.

items.title:
The Kling Report. For those interested in AI video.

I'm running a series of reports. Having AI read...
items.content:
The Kling Report. For those interested in AI video.

I'm running a series of reports. Having AI read my lists here on X (I have about 50,000 people in AI industry on them) looking for interesting trends. I've been having a ton of fun playing with Kling, so ran a report on that here:   

https://t.co/Y9wPU0gl79

Some things the AI I'm using (an enterprise grade cognitive architecture made by @blevlabs) found about Kling's competitive advantages:

Kling 3.0 — the most powerful commercial-grade AI video model. Native 1080p output, built for real advertising, brand storytelling, and professional film production.

Longest videos: 3 minutes vs 35-60 seconds for competitors

Multi-shot in single generation: No other model does 6 shots from one prompt

Native audio: Voice, dialogue, SFX, ambient, singing in one pass

Motion Control: Transfer motion from reference video (unique feature)

Pricing: Free tier with 66 daily credits; $6.99 entry point
Massive ecosystem: 8+ third-party platform integrations at launch

Scale: 60M users, 600M videos, $240M ARR — largest by adoption

Here's a fun video of some lobsters (thanks to @openclaw I'm into lobsters lately) that I made with Kling 3.0.

More on thread __>>

items.title: I would avoid downloading AI skills that have not been vetted by you through careful reading of the ...
items.content: I would avoid downloading AI skills that have not been vetted by you through careful reading of the markdown files (or, if outside your field, by an expert in that field you trust). There are obvious security risks, but there is even greater risk that the skill is just bad.

items.title:
We need coding agents that do both 
1. read 5-line spec -> generate entire product 0-1 models, an...
items.content:
We need coding agents that do both 
1. read 5-line spec -> generate entire product 0-1 models, and 
2. read 100k line code & 1M line logs -> 5 line change 

Pretty clear product UX & agent harness is more the problem than needing different weights

items.title:
🦠 We were interested in how the RCT results related to forecasters' views on biorisk.

We asked resp...
items.content:
🦠 We were interested in how the RCT results related to forecasters' views on biorisk.

We asked respondents to forecast their baseline risk of a large-scale human-caused outbreak in 2028.

The median forecaster gave a probability of 0.3%.

We then resurveyed participants after revealing the results of the RCT. The median across forecasters fell slightly to 0.2%

Among the most accurate forecasters, the median risk estimate fell from 0.18% to 0.10%
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI survey: median forecaster gave 0.3% baseline probability of large-scale human-caused outbreak by 2028, which fell slightly to 0.2% after seeing RCT results showing no LLM uplift
items.reasoning: Concrete biorisk forecasting data from experts, shows that negative RCT results (no LLM uplift) actually lowered perceived risk
items.summary_html: FRI survey: median forecaster gave 0.3% baseline probability of large-scale human-caused outbreak by 2028, which fell slightly to 0.2% after seeing RCT results showing no LLM uplift

items.title: @jkeatn This is what @blevlabs reported to me (cognitive AI that lets me search anything on X API): ...
items.content:
@jkeatn This is what @blevlabs reported to me (cognitive AI that lets me search anything on X API): Here are the best social scientists writing about AI right now — a mix of established academics, critical theorists, and embedded researchers:
The Critical/Justice Lens
Kate Crawford (@katecrawford) — Atlas of AI, USC/Microsoft Research. The definitive voice on AI's political economy, labor, and environmental cost
Safiya Noble (@safiyanoble) — Algorithms of Oppression, UCLA. Algorithmic bias and racial/gender harm in search and AI
Ruha Benjamin (@ruha9) — Race After Technology, Princeton. AI and structural racism
Timnit Gebru (@timnitGebru) — DAIR Institute. Bias in AI systems, led the famous Google exit; still the most cited voice in AI fairness
Virginia Eubanks — Automating Inequality. AI and class/poverty policy
The Mainstream Bridge-Builders
Ethan Mollick (@emollick) — Wharton. Best writer on how humans and AI actually work together. His Substack "One Useful Thing" is essential
Zeynep Tufekci (@zeynep) — Columbia. Technology and society, AI and democracy. New York Times columnist
danah boyd (@zephoria) — Data & Society. Long-running work on tech, youth, and social behavior
The Embedded Researchers (Inside Labs)
Hannah Wallach (@hannawallach) — Microsoft Research. Sociotechnical AI evaluation
Rishi Bommasani (@RishiBommasani) — Stanford HAI. AI's impact on human skills and society
Jessica Hullman (@JessicaHullman) — Northwestern. LLMs and behavioral/social science research methodology
Zoe Hitzig — left OpenAI; wrote that "we don't have a grasp on what AI does to people's psychology" — worth following
Up-and-Coming
Safiya Huang (@saffronhuang) — Anthropic Societal Impacts; researching how humans actually use AI agents
Jeremy Nguyen PhD (@JeremyNguyenPhD) — human workers + AI labor research, appearing frequently in your lists
Chinasa T. Okolo (@ChinasaTOkolo) — AI Now Institute; Global South AI perspectives
If I had to pick 3 to follow first: Ethan Mollick (most practical), Kate Crawford (most rigorous), Zeynep Tufekci (most readable for broad audiences).

items.title: For most of the 2010s, convolutional neural networks (ConvNets) — networks that process images by sl...
items.content:
For most of the 2010s, convolutional neural networks (ConvNets) — networks that process images by sliding small filters across them to detect patterns — were the dominant architecture for computer vision. Then transformers arrived.

Transformers, originally designed for text, process input by letting every element attend to every other element simultaneously, and when adapted for images they started outperforming ConvNets on benchmark after benchmark. The general consensus became that transformers were just better, and ConvNets were the old way.

This paper's authors decided to test whether that conclusion was actually justified, or whether ConvNets had simply fallen behind in terms of training recipes and design choices rather than fundamental capability.

They took a standard ResNet and systematically updated it — one change at a time — borrowing design decisions from transformers: things like which activation function to use, which type of normalization to apply, and how to reshape the internal dimensions of each processing block.

Each change was small and interpretable, and the paper tracks the accuracy at every step so you can see exactly what each decision contributes.

The final result is a pure ConvNet that matches or beats transformer-based models of equivalent size on image classification, object detection, and segmentation, by being up to 49% faster. 

Read with AI tutor: https://t.co/abh8jEt8A2

Read alone: https://t.co/PH5kBIpl70
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov explains the 'ConvNeXt' paper which systematically modernized a standard ResNet using transformer design choices, achieving matching/beating transformer performance on vision tasks while being up to 49% faster.
items.reasoning: Good educational content explaining an important paper on ConvNets vs Transformers. Well-written summary with strong engagement (43K views, 604 likes). Not new research but valuable explanation.
items.summary_html: Burkov explains the 'ConvNeXt' paper which systematically modernized a standard ResNet using transformer design choices, achieving matching/beating transformer performance on vision tasks while being up to 49% faster.
items.content_html:
For most of the 2010s, convolutional neural networks (ConvNets) — networks that process images by sliding small filters across them to detect patterns — were the dominant architecture for computer vision. Then transformers arrived.

Transformers, originally designed for text, process input by letting every element attend to every other element simultaneously, and when adapted for images they started outperforming ConvNets on benchmark after benchmark. The general consensus became that transformers were just better, and ConvNets were the old way.

This paper's authors decided to test whether that conclusion was actually justified, or whether ConvNets had simply fallen behind in terms of training recipes and design choices rather than fundamental capability.

They took a standard ResNet and systematically updated it — one change at a time — borrowing design decisions from transformers: things like which activation function to use, which type of normalization to apply, and how to reshape the internal dimensions of each processing block.

Each change was small and interpretable, and the paper tracks the accuracy at every step so you can see exactly what each decision contributes.

The final result is a pure ConvNet that matches or beats transformer-based models of equivalent size on image classification, object detection, and segmentation, by being up to 49% faster.

items.title:
Here's how the RCT worked:

🧑‍🔬 The RCT tested 153 novices' ability to complete three wet lab tasks:...
items.content:
Here's how the RCT worked:

🧑‍🔬 The RCT tested 153 novices' ability to complete three wet lab tasks:

Cell culture
Molecular cloning
Viral production

Participants had access to lab equipment and materials, but minimal instructions. They were randomly assigned to two groups:

🌐 Internet access only
🤖 Internet access + LLMs (from mid-2025)

The RCT would measure the percentage of novices in each group that could complete all three tasks. We asked forecasters to make their own predictions of these outcomes.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: Description of Active Site RCT methodology: 153 novices tested on cell culture, molecular cloning, and viral production tasks, randomized to internet-only vs internet+LLM groups
items.reasoning: Important methodological context for the biosecurity RCT, describes a well-designed experiment testing real LLM biorisk

items.title:
For many tasks, Claude Code is still my go-to. 

But here is one use case: I just used it as a task ...
items.content:
For many tasks, Claude Code is still my go-to. 

But here is one use case: I just used it as a task speed test, for something neither I nor my team have done before.

I asked Sonnet 4.6 while on a blank page - “hey go and research Google ad best practices and then create a paid google ad campaign for xyz with xyz vibe” and it did the whole thing and we came back to it and could scroll through and in about 20 seconds get a sense of site layout, task complexity, process, and best practices.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller shares her experience using Claude Code with Sonnet 4.6 for creating a Google ad campaign from scratch, noting it's useful as a task speed test for unfamiliar workflows.
items.reasoning: Practical first-hand experience report with Claude Sonnet 4.6 (released just 2 days prior on Feb 17). Provides real use case from a credible AI practitioner showing how coding agents can be used for non-coding tasks like ad campaign creation.
items.summary_html: Allie K Miller shares her experience using Claude Code with Sonnet 4.6 for creating a Google ad campaign from scratch, noting it's useful as a task speed test for unfamiliar workflows.
items.content_html:
For many tasks, Claude Code is still my go-to.

But here is one use case: I just used it as a task speed test, for something neither I nor my team have done before.

I asked Sonnet 4.6 while on a blank page - “hey go and research Google ad best practices and then create a paid google ad campaign for xyz with xyz vibe” and it did the whole thing and we came back to it and could scroll through and in about 20 seconds get a sense of site layout, task complexity, process, and best practices.

OpenRouter is a threat to your business. For more than two days now, the most popular image generation models aren't returning images when called via OpenRouter, and OpenRouter doesn't do anything.

Once your business is up and running, switch to direct calls to the original model providers. You will save 5% too.

items.title: Even today, given the right constraints and a bit of guidance on how to think about it, AIs can gene...
items.content:
Even today, given the right constraints and a bit of guidance on how to think about it, AIs can generate really good ideas across domains.

Plus if you have AIs generate dozens of ideas, have another AI grade them against a rubric, and choose the best, they get even better.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer arguing AIs can generate good ideas with proper constraints and guidance, and suggesting a meta-approach of generating many ideas, AI-grading them against a rubric, and selecting the best
items.reasoning: Practical technique for AI ideation from a respected AI builder. The generate-grade-select pipeline is a useful pattern.

items.content_html:
Even today, given the right constraints and a bit of guidance on how to think about it, AIs can generate really good ideas across domains.

Plus if you have AIs generate dozens of ideas, have another AI grade them against a rubric, and choose the best, they get even better.

We’re on a mission to parse the world’s hardest PDFs, and we’d love your help

There are so many doc...
items.content:
We’re on a mission to parse the world’s hardest PDFs, and we’d love your help

There are so many document types that introduce a million edge cases for current VLMs / OCR: handwritten forms, badly scanned/rotated pages, charts, diagrams, and more.

We are running a contest right now for you to try to extract the hardest PDFs you can find. Come sign up on our agent builder, describe what you want to extract through natural language, upload your document, and show the results.

If our platform doesn’t work, even better; this is great feedback for us to improve our service. Either way submit your project and we’d love to get your feedback!

Check out LlamaCloud here: https://t.co/XYZmx5TFz8

What if @elonmusk bought that to compete? If that happened everything would change. Startups can change the world quickly. Eight weeks ago no one knew what an @openclaw was.

Aside: I posted some sad news about a family member who is fighting cancer. Pulled it because I got yelled at for posting. The family hasn’t all learned yet.

Our family trip is getting cut short by bad weather across western USA. And big sadness.

Be nice to someone today. It might be your only chance.

items.title:
Nobody wants to use chatbots.

Most chatbots and voice AI agents I've seen are just glorified phone ...
items.content:
Nobody wants to use chatbots.

Most chatbots and voice AI agents I've seen are just glorified phone trees with better marketing.

These agents "understand" you just enough to transfer you to a human. They can answer basic questions but can't actually do anything.

I think this will change this year.

Every company in the world wants to deploy agents who don't simply hand off calls. These voice agents only make sense if they actually do stuff like:

• Handling a hotel reservation end-to-end
• Processing payments
• Giving details to customers without asking them tons of things
• Resolving issues

Check out @polyaivoice. They are currently handling 1M+ calls a day with AI.

PolyAI claims they can handle 40% of your call value on day one. Some of their clients are operating agents at the scale of 1,000+ humans!

Here is what blew my mind:

They evaluated the agents against humans on the hardest, most emotional calls. Agents scored higher in customer satisfaction.

That's not supposed to happen. And yet it is.

By the way, they just opened up the waitlist for Agent Wizard. You can use it to create a fully functioning Voice AI Agent for your company. No coding or setup call required.

This is the same tech behind Marriott, now available for everyone.

I think 2026 is the year when we won't need to talk to dumb phone trees anymore.

Thanks to the team for showing me their tech and collaborating with me on this post.

items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama discusses the current state of AI chatbots/voice agents, arguing most are glorified phone trees. Promotes PolyAI which handles 1M+ calls/day and claims higher customer satisfaction than humans on difficult calls.
items.reasoning: Interesting perspective on voice AI agent maturity but is partly sponsored content (PolyAI promotion). The 1M+ calls/day and human-beating satisfaction claims are noteworthy data points.

I set up Claude Code to play a human exhale every time it finishes a task. 

Why?
👂 It’s a helpful c...
items.content:
I set up Claude Code to play a human exhale every time it finishes a task. 

Why?
👂 It’s a helpful cue to look back at the screen 
👂 It’s more efficient when I’m managing multiple tasks 
👂 It’s a psychological hack - I almost automatically relax when I hear the noise 

Dozens of micro-relaxation cues a day that I don’t have to think about.

How to do it on a Mac: ask Claude Code to search pixabay (or something similar) for a short exhale sound effect, have it download it to your desktop, and add a stop hook to ~/.claude/settings.json that runs afplay on the file.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller shares a Claude Code productivity hack: setting up a human exhale sound to play when tasks complete, serving as attention cue, efficiency tool, and relaxation trigger.
items.reasoning: Creative and practical tip for AI coding workflow that speaks to the emerging UX of human-AI collaboration. Touches on psychological aspects of working alongside AI agents.
items.summary_html: Allie K Miller shares a Claude Code productivity hack: setting up a human exhale sound to play when tasks complete, serving as attention cue, efficiency tool, and relaxation trigger.
items.content_html:
I set up Claude Code to play a human exhale every time it finishes a task.

Why?

👂 It’s a helpful cue to look back at the screen

👂 It’s more efficient when I’m managing multiple tasks

👂 It’s a psychological hack - I almost automatically relax when I hear the noise

Dozens of micro-relaxation cues a day that I don’t have to think about.

How to do it on a Mac: ask Claude Code to search pixabay (or something similar) for a short exhale sound effect, have it download it to your desktop, and add a stop hook to ~/.claude/settings.json that runs afplay on the file.

items.title: I'm building a new kind of X news reader with @blevlabs' AI (which is better at analyzing X than any...
items.content:
I'm building a new kind of X news reader with @blevlabs' AI (which is better at analyzing X than any other AI that's available now).

One that reads all 150,000 posts going through my lists every day. 

If you had AGI, what would you want AGI to tell you about what's going on in X? 

By the way, my lists have a LOT more data than you might expect. Every part of tech industry is covered. And every part of world news is covered. 

It can even tell you what you should do with your investments based on what people are talking about when it comes to either private companies (startups) or public ones. 

If you could never read X directly again, and only could use your AI to read it, what would you tell your AI to bring you from X?

items.title:
It is still very clearly AI, though unsophisticated-on-AI users might be convinced it’s real. 

✅ It...
items.content:
It is still very clearly AI, though unsophisticated-on-AI users might be convinced it’s real. 

✅ It’s quite fast as responding (much faster than say ChatGPT live voice mode) but not as fast as a human. 

✅ It changes its tone very naturally but sometimes a bit exaggerated. 

✅ It stays on the same talk track, almost to a fault (ex: say the exact same thing over and over again and it only takes a few turns until every single response from the AI is the same text just with a different tone).
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller evaluates an AI voice product: faster than ChatGPT voice but not human-speed, natural tone changes but sometimes exaggerated, repetitive after a few turns.
items.reasoning: Useful qualitative assessment of AI voice technology limitations from a credible practitioner. Identifies specific failure modes like repetition loops.
items.summary_html: Allie K Miller evaluates an AI voice product: faster than ChatGPT voice but not human-speed, natural tone changes but sometimes exaggerated, repetitive after a few turns.
items.content_html:
It is still very clearly AI, though unsophisticated-on-AI users might be convinced it’s real.

✅ It’s quite fast as responding (much faster than say ChatGPT live voice mode) but not as fast as a human.

✅ It changes its tone very naturally but sometimes a bit exaggerated.

✅ It stays on the same talk track, almost to a fault (ex: say the exact same thing over and over again and it only takes a few turns until every single response from the AI is the same text just with a different tone).

Now, I'v...
items.content:
Back in 2012, I applied to six PhD programs, and had 0 calls before I received decisions. 

Now, I've heard of students doing up to 30 Zoom calls over the course of a PhD application season. Sounds exhausting.

Anyone have numbers for reference from their own experiences?

items.title: const quickSort = (a) => { if (a.length <= 1) return a; let p = a[a.length - 1], l = [], r = []; for...
items.content:
const quickSort = (a) => { if (a.length <= 1) return a; let p = a[a.length - 1], l = [], r = []; for (let i = 0; i < a.length - 1; i++) { a[i] < p ? l.push(a[i]) : r.push(a[i]); } return [...quickSort(l), p, ...quickSort(r)]; };

This is quick sort non-maintainable by a human and perfectly maintainable by an LLM.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares a compact quicksort implementation in JS, observing it's unmaintainable by humans but perfectly maintainable by LLMs.
items.reasoning: Interesting observation about how code maintainability standards may shift with LLM-assisted development. Provocative but light.
items.summary_html: Burkov shares a compact quicksort implementation in JS, observing it's unmaintainable by humans but perfectly maintainable by LLMs.
items.content_html:
const quickSort = (a) => { if (a.length <= 1) return a; let p = a[a.length - 1], l = [], r = []; for (let i = 0; i < a.length - 1; i++) { a[i] < p ? l.push(a[i]) : r.push(a[i]); } return [...quickSort(l), p, ...quickSort(r)]; };

This is quick sort non-maintainable by a human and perfectly maintainable by an LLM.

items.title: This workflow is based on determining how similar a request is to everything else the system has see...
items.content:
This workflow is based on determining how similar a request is to everything else the system has seen.

The crucial component in this workflow is a vector database that stores the system's experience.

That's where most of the magic happens!

@MongoDB offers native support for vector search. You can do everything with it.

Here is what you need to read:

1. AI Learning Hub: Free tutorials about embeddings, vector search, and agentic systems.

https://t.co/369bP8Xy5y

2. Python Development Series: workshop and webinars you can attend about how to build AI applications. They also cover everything you need to put together a workflow like the one here.

https://t.co/gNYtTvDNpL

3. MongoDB for Startups: offers free MongoDB Atlas credits, Voyage AI tokens, one-on-one technical advice, co-marketing opportunities, and access to their partner network.

https://t.co/1PG8vysWvk

Thanks to the MongoDB team for collaborating with me on this post.

items.summary: Svpino describing a production ML deployment strategy using vector databases for similarity-based request handling, promoting MongoDB's vector search capabilities
items.reasoning: Useful technical pattern but clearly sponsored content (MongoDB collaboration). The vector similarity routing strategy is practical but not novel.
items.summary_html: Svpino describing a production ML deployment strategy using vector databases for similarity-based request handling, promoting MongoDB's vector search capabilities

Security now means protecting the world from agents, and agents from the world.

items.title:
The practical framework:

→ visibility, know what agents are doing
→ security controls, limit what t...
items.content:
The practical framework:

→ visibility, know what agents are doing
→ security controls, limit what they can access
→ validation + guardrails, detect unsafe actions

Miss one, and you introduce systemic risk at scale.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon outlines a practical framework for AI agent safety: visibility, security controls, and validation/guardrails. Warns missing any one introduces systemic risk at scale.
items.reasoning: Reasonable AI agent safety framework but fairly generic. Low engagement.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon outlines a practical framework for AI agent safety: visibility, security controls, and validation/guardrails. Warns missing any one introduces systemic risk at scale.
items.content_html:
The practical framework:

→ visibility, know what agents are doing

→ security controls, limit what they can access

→ validation + guardrails, detect unsafe actions

Miss one, and you introduce systemic risk at scale.

items.title: @Math_MntnrHZ It's codine between two embeddings. Low similarly is expected given that a Unicode cha...
items.content:
@Math_MntnrHZ It's codine between two embeddings. Low similarly is expected given that a Unicode character doesn't provide much information compared the text. The goal isn't to give you one best option but several decent ones to choose from.

But you gave me an idea that I could ask a strong LLM to generate a short descriptive text for each emoji and then embed this text instead.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov discusses cosine similarity between embeddings for an emoji finder tool, considering using LLM-generated descriptions for better emoji embeddings.
items.reasoning: Minor technical discussion about embedding approaches, minimal engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov discusses cosine similarity between embeddings for an emoji finder tool, considering using LLM-generated descriptions for better emoji embeddings.
items.content_html:
@Math_MntnrHZ It's codine between two embeddings. Low similarly is expected given that a Unicode character doesn't provide much information compared the text. The goal isn't to give you one best option but several decent ones to choose from.

But you gave me an idea that I could ask a strong LLM to generate a short descriptive text for each emoji and then embed this text instead.

items.title: @DennisTheProf Thanks for the advice. I'm surprised that the techniques haven't changed since the 20...
items.content:
@DennisTheProf Thanks for the advice. I'm surprised that the techniques haven't changed since the 2010s.

Agree on sitemap.xml, breadcrumbs, expanded abstracts, and missed footers.

I doubt that infinite scroll is better than pagination. Is it based on any experiments or it's your personal experience?

I found my H1s already descriptive of the content. New Stuff is a single exception I found, so I don't think that replacing it with something ugly but bot-friendly woulnd't help much.

Why "Googles Machine Learning Guides" is better than "Googles machine learning guides"? This capitalization is used in the US and not in other English-speaking countries. Does Google put so much weight on this?

Agreed on descriptive URLs, add meta titles and meta descriptions.

Not sure about this one: "Google Cant See JavaScript on first crawl, it wont return for second crawl with this little info." It's a React website, I don't control the static info: the framework always uses the same index.html and its content is rendered dynamically by JavaScript. If as you say Google doesn't see enough to return, then none of the React websites would be indexed. Are there really SEO tricks that apply to the static HTML of a React website that make Google bot return?

Coffee bought 👍
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov discusses SEO optimization for a React website, asking about JavaScript rendering, infinite scroll vs pagination, and URL capitalization conventions.
items.reasoning: Off-topic SEO discussion, not relevant to AI/ML.
items.summary_html: Burkov discusses SEO optimization for a React website, asking about JavaScript rendering, infinite scroll vs pagination, and URL capitalization conventions.

I doubt that infinite scroll is better than pagination. Is it based on any experiments or it's your personal experience?

I found my H1s already descriptive of the content. New Stuff is a single exception I found, so I don't think that replacing it with something ugly but bot-friendly woulnd't help much.

Why "Googles Machine Learning Guides" is better than "Googles machine learning guides"? This capitalization is used in the US and not in other English-speaking countries. Does Google put so much weight on this?

Agreed on descriptive URLs, add meta titles and meta descriptions.

Not sure about this one: "Google Cant See JavaScript on first crawl, it wont return for second crawl with this little info." It's a React website, I don't control the static info: the framework always uses the same index.html and its content is rendered dynamically by JavaScript. If as you say Google doesn't see enough to return, then none of the React websites would be indexed. Are there really SEO tricks that apply to the static HTML of a React website that make Google bot return?
